textPlain: Nice achievement, building on the foregone dreams of Lisp powered workstations.We need more efforts like these and less yet another UNIX clone in C.

This looks nice and very interesting that this runs on bare metal. Is more documentation / tutorials available anywhere?EDIT: Found the documentation:
https://scheme.fail/manual/loko.html
I also like that it runs on bare metal. I wonder is it builds and runs on macOS? (I am on a mobile device and can’t check it myself.)Off topic, but I love the design of the linked web site.

> Loko’s runtime uses concurrency based on Concurrent ML.That one could be a big deal.https://scheme.fail/manual/loko.html#ConcurrencyThe docs don't mention channels and say that fibres are built on limited continuations (call/cc) which suggests missing some of the clever stuff in CML (e.g. that threads deadlocked on a channel get garbage collected)
This is super interesting. I kind of want to use this to turn one of my old laptops into a Scheme machine. But from the docs I think on bare metal it's only usable over serial.
Agree. How would one use/access graphics and GUIs with overlapping windows from it?

After skimming the docs and the site, I suspect this is x86-only for now. No mention of ARM or other processor architectures and the listed hardware support suggests a PC hardware target. You could probably cross compile on an Apple silicon Mac and run under emulation (e.g., QEMU).

While being heavily inspired guile-fibers, it seems to not actually be parallel.Anyway, I would say it is actually the nicest way to write concurrent programs. It supports you and helps you to not shoot yourself in the foot, while also staying out of the way.

I did successfully run the bare-metal Loko image on the ThinkPad x230
with GUI, mouse and keyboard access.
